sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
Chief Complaint
Persistent coughing and wheezing related to asthma symptoms.
History of Present Illness
The patient reports a long-standing history of asthma, characterized by recurring episodes of coughing and wheezing, particularly triggered by colds and exposure to allergens. He notes that his asthma symptoms have worsened over the past six months, leading to frequent urgent care visits and primary care visits. He reports a flare-up after visiting his aunt's house due to her cat, and has had several ER visits this past year, often requiring prednisone. He uses an albuterol inhaler frequently and has tried a second inhaler, but it doesn't seem to work for him. He's been using it almost every day. He enjoys hiking and is looking forward to getting back to it.
Findings
Blood pressure is 128/82 mmHg. Respiratory rate is 16 breaths per minute. Oxygen saturation is 99% on room air. Heart sounds are regular and rhythmic. Lung exam reveals diminished breath sounds and occasional slight expiratory wheeze bilaterally. Pulmonary function test results are consistent with a diagnosis of asthma. Strong bilateral pulses, brisk capillary refill, and no clubbing or edema noted. Neck is supple, trachea midline.
Assessment

sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
Moderate to intermediate intermittent asthma. The patient's symptoms are consistent with a poorly controlled asthma, contributing to frequent exacerbations and emergency room visits.
Plan
Start Flovent 100 mcg twice daily. Continue using the albuterol inhaler PRN as needed, but wait to see if breathing improves before taking additional puffs. Develop and provide an asthma action plan. Schedule a follow-up appointment in 30 days to assess response to treatment and adjust medication as needed. Advise the patient to avoid exposure to known triggers, such as cats, and to continue with regular exercise, being mindful of his asthma symptoms.
